vitessio/vitess#18786.body:
## Feature Description

The flow-based balancer introduced in #16351 works well for deployments where query traffic is evenly distributed across all cells containing VTGates. However, it makes a fundamental assumption that may not hold in all topologies: that each cell with a VTGate receives an equal share of inbound application traffic. 

In practice, many deployments have uneven traffic distribution across cells.

In these cases, the current model breaks down because it assumes uniform traffic distribution. The balancer calculates flows based on equal input from each VTGate cell, but if most queries actually originate from VTGates in a single cell, the other cells' tablets will be underutilized while attempting to maintain the calculated global balance. Consider this example:

Application deployment:
-  Cell A: 90% of application traffic --> VTGate A
 - Cell B: 10% of application traffic --> VTGate B
 - Cell C: 0% of application traffic (no VTGate, or unused)

Database deployment:
 - Cell A: 1 replica
 - Cell B: 1 replica  
 - Cell C: 1 replica

With the flow-based balancer configured with all three cells, each VTGate attempts to balance load equally across all tablets. However, since 90% of queries come through Cell A's VTGate, the tablet in Cell A still receives the majority of the load despite the balancer's efforts. A random strategy:

- Ignores cell affinity completely
- Selects tablets with uniform probability (1/N for N available tablets)
- Makes no assumptions about traffic distribution across VTGate cells
- Provides predictable load distribution regardless of application deployment topology
- The random mode trades off potential latency benefits from cell affinity for guaranteed even load distribution. 

This may be the right tradeoff when:
- Application traffic is concentrated in fewer cells than database replicas
- Cross-cell latency is acceptable for the workload
- Avoiding replica hotspots is more important than minimizing latency
vitessio/vitess#18786.title: RFC: Random Tablet Balancer Mode

vitessio/vitess#18804.body:
### Overview of the Issue

There are two lag related values provided in the `show` output of a VReplication workflow: 
  - max_v_replication_lag - the max time since the last event was processed across all of the streams (liveness)
  - max_v_replication_transaction_lag - the max statement processing lag across all of the streams (logical source_commit_trx_N --> target_commit_trx_N lag)

While the v_replication_lag value offers liveness indicators and can highlight some issues, it's the v_replication_transaction_lag value that most people are interested in and it better reflects how far behind the target is from the source in the stream of events.

In VTAdmin, the value for measuring lag (both on the workflow landing page, and the details page) is max_v_replication_lag which may not be as helpful in troubleshooting issues; and could even be misleading if people interpret the information incorrectly. VTAdmin can make it appear that the workflow is fully caught up while the target is really lagged behind several hours. You would see this fuller picture when looking at the `show` client command output, but not in the VTAdmin views.

### Reproduction Steps

```
git checkout main && make build

./101_initial_cluster.sh && ./201_customer_tablets.sh && ./202_move_tables.sh

# In another terminal window, to generate a constant set of transactions on the source
while true; do mysql commerce -e "insert into customer values ($RANDOM*$RANDOM, 'mlord@planetscale.com')"; done

# In yet another terminal window, to monitor the liveness lag and the transaction lag
watch -n1 -d "vtctldclient --server :15999 --compact movetables --target-keyspace customer --workflow commerce2customer show --include-logs=false | grep -E 'lag'"

# Stop the workflow for a while so that we develop some lag
vtctldclient --server :15999 --compact movetables --target-keyspace customer --workflow commerce2customer stop
sleep 300

# Allow it to start catching up, but slow also slow the catchup process down
while true; do vtctldclient --server :15999 --compact movetables --target-keyspace customer --workflow commerce2customer stop; sleep 10; vtctldclient --server :15999 --compact movetables --target-keyspace customer --workflow commerce2customer start; done
```

If you look in VTAdmin after the last step, you'll never see the vreplication lag go above 10 seconds. That mirrors what you see in the terminal window running the show command. Here's example output that highlights the difference:
```
Every 1.0s: vtctldclient --server :15999 --compact movetables --target-keyspace customer --workflow commerce2customer show --… pslord.local: 21:15:22
                                                                                                                                        in 0.055s (0)
      "max_v_replication_lag": "2",
      "max_v_replication_transaction_lag": "249",
```

There's only ever at most about a 10 second liveness lag — the time when the workflow is stopped and we're not receiving any events — but the transaction lag is much higher and slowly starts to come down over time. And logically, the primary thing we care about when showing the logical lag in the workflow is the transaction lag. That is what reflects whether or not the workflow is able to keep up with the writes on the source and if it's currently lagging behind.

And if you look at the VTAdmin workflows panel/view and the details tab for our workflow there, you will see the lag value that's shown is the v_replication_lag and it does not accurately reflect how far behind the workflow is: meaning how far the target is behind the source in processing the transactions as they come in.

### Binary Version

```sh
vtgate version Version: 24.0.0-SNAPSHOT
```

### Operating System and Environment details

```sh
N/A
```

### Log Fragments

```sh

```
vitessio/vitess#18804.title: Bug Report: VTAdmin workflow detail page uses max_v_replication_lag value which can be misleading

vitessio/vitess#18805.body:
### Overview of the Issue

This can happen in the following scenario with the [`ReplicationTracker`](https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/blob/main/go/vt/vttablet/tabletserver/repltracker/repltracker.go):
1. You start a [PRS](https://vitess.io/docs/reference/programs/vtctldclient/vtctldclient_plannedreparentshard/) while the replicas are all lagging
2. We demote the current primary, which [stops the heartbeat writer on the old primary and blocks any new writes](https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/blob/d02d40cebf0c2ddfd7ba64b002e629a2315ea048/go/vt/vttablet/tabletserver/state_manager.go#L517-L518)
3. We select the best new primary candidate — which was a replica — and proceed to wait for all replicas to catchup 
4. They catch up within the timeout window and the PRS moves forward (eventually succeeding)
5. The new primary [goes into primary mode and the tracker's heartbeat reader is stopped](https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/blob/d02d40cebf0c2ddfd7ba64b002e629a2315ea048/go/vt/vttablet/tabletserver/repltracker/repltracker.go#L93-L97)
6. At step 2 the previous/old primary stopped sending heartbeats and blocked any new writes. So after that point we are not able to update the new primary's replication lag in the tracker — because no new heartbeats are coming through — to reflect the fact that it got down to 0 in step 3. So the new/current primary continues to [export a metric](https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/blob/d02d40cebf0c2ddfd7ba64b002e629a2315ea048/go/vt/vttablet/tabletserver/repltracker/poller.go#L31) which errantly reflects that it currently has 10s of replica lag as the metric variable is no longer [getting updated here](https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/blob/d02d40cebf0c2ddfd7ba64b002e629a2315ea048/go/vt/vttablet/tabletserver/repltracker/poller.go#L70) because that [poller call is no longer made by the tracker when it's running on a primary](https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/blob/d02d40cebf0c2ddfd7ba64b002e629a2315ea048/go/vt/vttablet/tabletserver/repltracker/repltracker.go#L131-L138)

This can then cause confusing graphs and errant alerts based on the stale replication lag value for the tablet.

### Reproduction Steps

You can follow the steps noted above. Skipping a repeatable test case here as you can see how the issue can happen by walking the code path.

### Binary Version

```sh
vtgate version Version: 24.0.0-SNAPSHOT (Git revision a8edcbc4542c8f9826b0b8fe3d573c057f0b7ecb branch 'vtadmin_use_vrepl_trx_lag') built on Fri Oct 24 21:05:26 UTC 2025 by matt@pslord.local using go1.25.2 darwin/arm64
```

### Operating System and Environment details

```sh
N/A
```

### Log Fragments

```sh

```
vitessio/vitess#18805.title: Bug Report: ReplicationTracker can errantly report lag on a primary

vitessio/vitess#18808.body:
# RFC: Tablet-Specific Routing in VTGate

## Problem Statement

Vitess users need a way to route queries to specific tablets for operational and observability purposes. Currently, VTGate routes queries based on keyspace, shard, and tablet type (PRIMARY, REPLICA, RDONLY), but there's no standardized way to target an individual tablet within a shard.

### Current Limitations

- **Monitoring Tools**: Database monitoring and profiling tools often need to maintain persistent connections to specific tablets to collect per-tablet metrics accurately. Without tablet-specific routing, metrics get aggregated or inconsistently attributed across tablet replicas.

- **Troubleshooting**: When investigating performance issues or debugging query behavior on a specific tablet, operators have no simple way to send test queries directly to that tablet through VTGate.

- **Operational Tasks**: Tasks like warming up a specific replica's cache, testing a particular tablet's behavior, or validating tablet-specific configurations require workarounds or direct MySQL connections, bypassing VTGate's connection pooling and query rewriting.

## Use Cases

1. **Per-Tablet Observability**: Monitoring tools that collect query performance metrics, slow query logs, or profiling data need to maintain stable connections to individual tablets to ensure metrics are correctly attributed.

2. **Targeted Debugging**: When a specific tablet exhibits unusual behavior (high CPU, slow queries, lock contention), operators need to send diagnostic queries directly to that tablet to investigate.

3. **Selective Testing**: Before promoting a replica to primary or after configuration changes, operators may want to test queries against a specific tablet without affecting production traffic patterns.

4. **Cache Warming**: After a tablet restart or failover, operators may want to send specific queries to warm up that tablet's query cache or buffer pool.

5. **Development & QA**: Developers testing sharded applications may need to target specific tablets to verify data distribution or test edge cases.

## Proposed Solution

Extend VTGate's `USE` statement syntax to support tablet-specific routing:

```sql
USE commerce:-80@primary|zone1-0000000100;
```

Where `tablet-alias` follows the standard Vitess format: `cell-uid` (e.g., `zone1-0000000100`).

### Behavior

- **Sticky Connection**: Once set, all subsequent queries in the session route to the specified tablet until cleared or changed
- **Clear Targeting**: Standard `USE keyspace` or `USE keyspace@tablet-type` clears tablet-specific routing

### Syntax Examples

```sql
-- Target a specific tablet
USE commerce:-80@primary|zone1-0000000100;
SELECT * FROM products;  -- Routes to zone1-0000000100

-- Transactions work as expected
BEGIN;
INSERT INTO orders VALUES (...);
COMMIT;  -- All operations go to the same tablet

-- Clear tablet targeting, return to normal routing
USE commerce;
SELECT * FROM products;  -- Routes normally (load-balanced across replicas)

-- Switch to tablet type routing
USE commerce@replica;
SELECT * FROM products;  -- Routes to any replica (clears tablet-specific routing)
```

- Vitess already supports shard-specific routing: `USE keyspace:shard@tablet_type`
- Transactions already use tablet-specific routing internally via `shardSession.TabletAlias`
- This feature extends existing patterns to expose tablet-level control to users
vitessio/vitess#18808.title: RFC: Tablet Targeting

vitessio/vitess#18843.body:
### Feature Description

Today the workflow `status` sub-command (e.g. [`MoveTables`](https://vitess.io/docs/reference/programs/vtctldclient/vtctldclient_movetables/vtctldclient_movetables_status/)) will include copy progress information when the workflow is in [the copy phase](https://vitess.io/docs/reference/vreplication/internal/life-of-a-stream/#modes-in-detail).

The information provided is, however, incomplete. That is because it only provides information for tables in the workflow _for which we have not completed the copy phase_. So when monitoring the overall progress of the copy phase for the workflow it is more difficult to understand fully what work has been completed so far and how far along we are in total -- which is in part based on how many tables we've finished (and their relative size) and how many are left (and again, their relative size). Then you could estimate e.g. that it's taken us X minutes to get ~ Y% of the way in the workflow, so it should finish in ~ Z minutes.

It would be nice if we included details for every table in the workflow so that you get a more complete picture of how far along we are in the copy phase. Then you can know not only how far along percentage-wise we are for the current table being copied, but you can get an idea percentage-wise how far along the workflow itself is in the copy phase.

### Use Case(s)

Improved monitoring and observability.
vitessio/vitess#18843.title: Feature Request: VReplication copy progress in workflow status is incomplete

vitessio/vitess#18846.body:
### Feature Description

Sometimes filtering through backups outside of vitess can be complicated when all we have to identify the tablet that created a backup is the TabletAlias. So I would like to propose adding the Hostname as well there for completion and to make it easier to filter backups when not necessarily being used by Vitess APIs

### Use Case(s)

We would be able to filter through backups created by a particular hostname, not just the TabletAlias, making it easier to find the right backup in some cases.
vitessio/vitess#18846.title: Feature Request: Add Hostname to the `BackupManifest`

vitessio/vitess#18850.body:
### Overview of the Issue

The bug is caused by this [logic](https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/blob/8fa9b87e4459b0e51694105abcdf6df5d04fccd7/go/vt/vtgate/vstream_manager.go#L724-L727). We buffer with eventss the entire transaction before sending it. Very large transactions eg multi-GB can cause OOM errors. Example described [here](https://vitess.slack.com/archives/C0PQY0PTK/p1761775363795949?thread_ts=1761693830.022079&cid=C0PQY0PTK)

it was from an 11GB size transaction (11 MB per row * 1000 rows). So it seems there was some issue with breaking this transaction up between tablet to vtgate and vtgate to client. We are still looking into it, but wanted to share early findings

### Reproduction Steps

Create a transaction that exceeds the memory limit of the vtgate. It will cause it to OOM. If you set GOMEMLIMIT it will help (GC's the cloned ROW events) or use exclude keyspace from table name to avoid cloning.

### Binary Version

```sh
19.0.7
```

### Operating System and Environment details

```sh
vitess@vtgate-bedrock-cdc-dev-iad-7f96cfb9fb-8dxn8:/vt/bin$ cat /etc/os-release
PRETTY_NAME="Ubuntu 22.04.5 LTS"
NAME="Ubuntu"
VERSION_ID="22.04"
VERSION="22.04.5 LTS (Jammy Jellyfish)"
VERSION_CODENAME=jammy
ID=ubuntu
ID_LIKE=debian
HOME_URL="https://www.ubuntu.com/"
SUPPORT_URL="https://help.ubuntu.com/"
BUG_REPORT_URL="https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/"
PRIVACY_POLICY_URL="https://www.ubuntu.com/legal/terms-and-policies/privacy-policy"
UBUNTU_CODENAME=jammy
```

### Log Fragments

```sh
From VStream client: Network closed for unknown reason
```
vitessio/vitess#18850.title: Bug Report: VTGates OOM on large transactions

vitessio/vitess#18851.body:
### Overview of the Issue

If you have a PK on a table like:

```sql
CREATE TABLE `datums` (
	`user_id` varchar(64) NOT NULL,
	`data` varbinary(2500) NOT NULL,
         ....
	PRIMARY KEY (`user_id`, `key_prefix`)
```

If you use the full `data`, your `lastpk` will overflow the `varbinary(2000)` that it is in `copy_state`, which will cause the OnlineDDL to fail. 

### Reproduction Steps

Create a table with a huge PK and attempt to do OnlineDDL against it

### Binary Version

```sh
Vitess 22, though this will fail on all version of Vitess.
```

### Operating System and Environment details

```sh
Ubuntu 24.04 in Kube
```

### Log Fragments

```sh
vreplication: terminal error: task error: error updating _vt.copy_state: Data too long for column 'lastpk' at row 1 (errno 1406) (sqlstate 22001) during query: insert into _vt.copy_state (lastpk, vrepl_id, table_name) values ...
```
vitessio/vitess#18851.title: Bug Report: `copy_state` col `lastpk` is too small for big PKs

vitessio/vitess#18885.body:
### Overview of the Issue

The semi-sync monitor was added in https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/pull/17763. It tries to ensure that when there are no other writes happening, it will generate its own heartbeat writes in order to try and unblock semi-sync. Semi-sync is blocked when the last write is waiting for a semi-sync ACK indefinitely because at the time of that write on the primary there were no replicas available to ACK it. This can be unblocked by performing another write when there are subsequently replicas available to ACK (a later ACK unblocks any previous writes waiting for an ACK as the ACK of the later GTID means the earlier ones were also received by that replica).

The initial implementation has two major issues, however, both of which cause problems in write heavy and heavily loaded replica sets where the system as a whole can become overloaded (TCP retries, packet loss, etc) which causes the timing of events to become atypical due to these delays:
  1. When [determining if semi-sync is blocked](https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/blob/d671bafcf0533c3895d90ee35a4dfd1f259aed10/go/vt/vttablet/tabletmanager/semisyncmonitor/monitor.go#L40), it only [looks to see if there are 1 or more connections/sessions waiting for a semi-sync ACK](https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/blob/d671bafcf0533c3895d90ee35a4dfd1f259aed10/go/vt/vttablet/tabletmanager/semisyncmonitor/monitor.go#L199-L225). This is wrong. It's not unusual to be waiting for an ACK for a brief period of time when the replicas are heavily loaded and lagging. Waiting != hung/blocked. It only means we're waiting 🙂. We need to see if there are still successful ACKs happening as that means that we're progressing and while we may have been / are waiting for an ACK in 1 or more sessions, we ARE making progress and thus we are NOT blocked.
  2. When we believe that semi-sync is blocked, we [start sending writes](https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/blob/d671bafcf0533c3895d90ee35a4dfd1f259aed10/go/vt/vttablet/tabletmanager/semisyncmonitor/monitor.go#L291-L311) — up to 15, each one in its own connection/session, each one [doing an INSERT into the `_vt.semisync_heartbeat` table](https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/blob/d671bafcf0533c3895d90ee35a4dfd1f259aed10/go/vt/vttablet/tabletmanager/semisyncmonitor/monitor.go#L344-L370) — until one of them goes through. During this time we never re-check to see if semi-sync is unblocked. The monitor assumes that there are no other writes happening outside of the monitor (from the user/app) that DID make it through during this time. And because the system is likely already overloaded, these new writes can themselves be delayed, themselves block, and cause further issues. 
      - The monitor itself could cause itself to become unable to function and cause the errant ERS in this way
           -  The primary has an unreliable network connection to the replicas for a period, this then causes the issue noted in point 1 
           -  The first INSERT it does blocks waiting for an ACK it won't receive
           - The ["cleanup" ticker](https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/blob/d671bafcf0533c3895d90ee35a4dfd1f259aed10/go/vt/vttablet/tabletmanager/semisyncmonitor/monitor.go#L151) fires ([every 24 hours](https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/blob/d671bafcf0533c3895d90ee35a4dfd1f259aed10/go/vt/vttablet/tabletmanager/semisyncmonitor/monitor.go#L44)) — which keeps the size of the `_vt.semisync_heartbeat` table minimal — and the TRUNCATE is executed, which tries to get a table level lock and blocks behind the INSERT (so we never even attempt to commit and request an ACK)
           -  All subsequent INSERT statements that the monitor runs to try and unblock things — remember, it's no longer checking to see if it was otherwise unblocked outside of the monitor and it will not check again until [`--semi-sync-monitor-interval`](https://vitess.io/docs/23.0/reference/programs/vttablet/) ([defaults to 10s](https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/blob/d671bafcf0533c3895d90ee35a4dfd1f259aed10/go/vt/vttablet/tabletmanager/semisyncmonitor/monitor.go#L100)) is hit again — and these INSERTs block behind the TRUNCATE
           - We then hit the [15 writers limit](https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/blob/d671bafcf0533c3895d90ee35a4dfd1f259aed10/go/vt/vttablet/tabletmanager/semisyncmonitor/monitor.go#L43) and the `FullStatus` `vttablet` [RPC response to `vtorc` indicates that semi-sync is stuck](https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/blob/d671bafcf0533c3895d90ee35a4dfd1f259aed10/go/vt/vttablet/tabletmanager/rpc_replication.go#L190) and it should perform an ERS to unblock the shard
           - In the end, we end up telling `vtorc` that semi-sync is fully blocked and it needs to do an ERS — when in reality everything is fine (just experiencing a reasonable amount of delay in the system). The behavior in this scenario is worse than if we had no monitor at all.

So today the monitor is ideal for replica sets with very low write rates, but potentially harmful for replica sets with high write rates. 

### Reproduction Steps

See code walkthrough.

### Binary Version

```sh
vtgate version Version: 24.0.0-SNAPSHOT (Git revision 3d2cdc546a6ac223866e0287782e8b3912efe2ca branch 'improve_semi-sync_monitor') built on Fri Nov  7 05:42:51 UTC 2025 by matt@pslord.local using go1.25.3 darwin/arm64
```

### Operating System and Environment details

```sh
N/A
```

### Log Fragments

```sh

```
vitessio/vitess#18885.title: Bug Report: Encoded Semi-Sync Heartbeat Monitor Behavior Can Lead to Errant ERS

vitessio/vitess#18886.body:
### Feature Description

There are various scenarios where it would be really nice to be able to specify a set of SQL queries to run just prior to initializing the backup of a tablet.

The user would provide an optional list of backup init SQL queries to run before taking the backup, the tablet types to run them on, when to timeout that work, and whether or not to fail the backup if we are unable to perform the work (including if we hit the timeout).

### Use Case(s)

For example, this could be used to run OPTIMIZE TABLE on 1 or more tables which you know will benefit from that. This example can improve the table copy time (to backup storage), reduce the storage used for the table in the backup, and improve the restore time for the table as well.

It could be similar to MySQL's init options
  - --init-file: https://dev.mysql.com/doc/refman/8.4/en/server-system-variables.html#sysvar_init_file
  - --init-connect: https://dev.mysql.com/doc/refman/8.4/en/server-system-variables.html#sysvar_init_connect
vitessio/vitess#18886.title: Feature Request: Add support for executing SQL queries prior to taking a backup

vitessio/vitess#18888.body:
### Feature Description

Allow `vtcombo` and `vttestserver` to configure the vtgate flag `gateway-initial-tablet-timeout`, which currently defaults to 30 seconds. From local experiments, setting a low `gateway-initial-tablet-timeout` value (e.g. 1s) appears to massively reduce `vttestserver` start-up time (by ~30s). This seems to work well because in a `vttestserver` environment, keyspaces and tablets are typically quick to start up.

### Use Case(s)

At Block, we heavily use `vttestserver` in all our Vitess apps' integration tests. We maintain an open-source Java / Kotlin library component named [VitessTestDb](https://github.com/cashapp/misk/tree/d6615f3d03c54d4e49f0d06c27d273b9bdb7be90/misk-vitess#vitesstestdb) that wraps `vttestserver`.

It's been a goal of ours to reduce container start-up time where possible to reduce overall build times in CI, and potentially build towards a `testcontainers` implementation in the future (which would be language agnostic and support more than just Java / Kotlin), and this solution would work best with fast creation of containers. 

Configuring `gateway-initial-tablet-timeout` to a low value seems like a quick win to reduce start-up time. Let me know however if there are better solutions out there.
vitessio/vitess#18888.title: Feature Request: add gateway-initial-tablet-timeout flag to vttest

vitessio/vitess#18889.body:
### Overview of the Issue

If you specify the keyspace name in the foreign key `CONSTRAINT`'s `REFERENCES` clause then this gets pushed down to `mysqld` as-is and it fails because by default the database name in `mysqld` is `vt_<keyspace_name>`. 

### Reproduction Steps

```
git checkout main && make build

cd examples/local
./101_initial_cluster.sh

mysql -e "CREATE TABLE commerce.departments (dept_id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY, dept_name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL)"
```

vitessio/vitess#18914.body:
### Feature Description

As a follow-up to https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/pull/5251, where a simpler algorithm for lag detection was added to VTGate, this issue proposes the full deprecation of the `--legacy-replication-lag-algorithm` flag, which seems to be the original intent of https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/pull/5251 about 6 years ago

`--legacy-replication-lag-algorithm=false` is currently in use at some of the largest Vitess deployments _(5 years+ at Slack)_ and has been proven to be more stable. There is also a desire from the maintainers to remove the old code

This deprecation must happen in 3 x phases:
1. `v24` - change default to `false`, warn about future deprecation in changelog
    - PR: https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/pull/18915
3. `v25` - deprecate the flag, make it cause a no-op
    - PR: TBD
5. `v26` - remove the flag 🧹
    - PR: TBD
 
### Use Case(s)

General users of VTGate
vitessio/vitess#18914.title: Feature Request: deprecate the `--legacy-replication-lag-algorithm` VTGate flag

vitessio/vitess#18930.body:
### Summary

We are experiencing constant throttling of vreplication flows when Vitess Tablet Throttler is enabled on a large cluster (20 replicas across 2 regions). As soon as throttling is turned on via the `UpdateThrottlerConfig` gRPC endpoint, vreplication stalls and never recovers. The error persists indefinitely until throttling is disabled.
```
reason_throttled: vplayer:mv_fix_1:vreplication is denied access due to unexpected error: metric not collected yet
```

### Environment
- Large Vitess cluster with ~20 replicas
   - Region A: 10 replicas
   - Region B: 10 replicas (50–70 ms cross-region latency)
-Reference keyspace replicating into the main keyspace
- Throttler enabled via `UpdateThrottlerConfig`
- `activeCollectInterval`: 250 ms

### Observed Behavior

- Once throttling is enabled, vreplication stops progressing. _vt.vreplication shows repeated errors:
```
reason_throttled: vplayer:mv_fix_1:vreplication is denied access due to unexpected error: metric not collected yet
```

- Running vtctldclient GetThrottlerStatus shows:
  -  _Self-scope metrics are collected correctly._
  - _**Shard-scope metrics consistently fail with metric not collected yet.**_

**The error never clears. Disabling throttling immediately restores vreplication to normal behavior.**

### Expected Behavior

Throttler metric collection should complete within the 250 ms collection _(maybe a long shot for such large clusters)_ window or degrade gracefully without blocking vreplication. vreplication should not stall indefinitely.

### Reproduction Steps

- Run a cluster with 20 replicas across 2 regions.
- Start vreplication into this keyspace.
- Enable the throttler using UpdateThrottlerConfig.

#### Observe that:

- vreplication stalls,
- shard-scope metrics fail permanently,
- errors persist until throttler is fully disabled.


### Suspected Root Cause [We are still debugging if the following points help in fixing the issue or not]

#### 1. Cluster size + cross-region latency exceeds collection window
- Shard-scope metric collection requires the PRIMARY to reach out to all 20 replicas every 250 ms.
https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/blob/a5865903f7d12dc39b3156d8f67d1a07dcc79e87/go/vt/vttablet/tabletserver/throttle/throttler.go#L85 
- Cross-region replicas add 50–70 ms latency for half of these calls.
- We suspect the 250 ms loop is insufficient for 20 RPCs including cross-region calls, creating constant backlog and continuous “metric not collected yet” failures.

### 2. grpcctmclient concurrency bottleneck (mutex contention)

We suspect issues with go/vt/vttablet/grpctmclient/client.go:

- Single mutex protecting unrelated structures, one mutex (client.mu) guarded:
  -rpcClientMap (buffered channel pool)
  -rpcDialPoolMap (dedicated connection pool)
https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/blob/a5865903f7d12dc39b3156d8f67d1a07dcc79e87/go/vt/vttablet/grpctmclient/client.go#L107-L117

#### Implications:

- During tablet inventory refresh or startup, dial-pool initialization can hold the mutex.
- Meanwhile, throttler metric collection calls (CheckThrottler) block on this mutex.
- These calls hit the 1 s timeout, causing repeated “metric not collected yet” errors.
- Increasing tablet_manager_grpc_concurrency (e.g., from 8 → 64) may reduce pressure but does not fix the root problem.

#### Possible Fix: 
- in  `go/vt/vttablet/grpctmclient/client.go` have :
```
	// poolMu protects rpcClientMap, dedicatedMu protects rpcDialPoolMap.
	poolMu         sync.Mutex
	dedicatedMu    sync.Mutex
```
- use `poolMu` for `dialPool` and `dedidcatedMu` for `dialDedicatedPool`

 ### 3. Remaining bottleneck after separating poolMu and dedicatedMu

Even after separating the mutexes:
- dialDedicatedPool still holds dedicatedMu while executing network I/O inside createTmc().
- When PRIMARY collects metrics from 20+ replicas, it spawns many goroutines.
- All of them try calling dialDedicatedPool, but serialization on dedicatedMu causes:
  - Wait times exceeding several seconds,
  - gRPC context deadline exceeded (1 s)
  - Continuous “metric not collected yet” throttler errors.
- Since the collector runs every 250 ms but each cycle can take multiple seconds, this leads to a permanent backlog and never-ending throttling.

#### Possible fix:
- Add the following code to `grpctmclient/client.go`
```
type tmcEntry struct {
	once sync.Once
	tmc  *tmc
	err  error
}

type addrTmcMap map[string]*tmcEntry
```
- Change the code in `dialDedicatedPool` to : 
```
func (client *grpcClient) dialDedicatedPool(ctx context.Context, dialPoolGroup DialPoolGroup, tablet *topodatapb.Tablet) (tabletmanagerservicepb.TabletManagerClient, invalidatorFunc, error) {
	addr := netutil.JoinHostPort(tablet.Hostname, int32(tablet.PortMap["grpc"]))
	opt, err := grpcclient.SecureDialOption(cert, key, ca, crl, name)
	if err != nil {
		return nil, nil, err
	}

	client.dedicatedMu.Lock()
	if client.rpcDialPoolMap == nil {
		client.rpcDialPoolMap = make(map[DialPoolGroup]addrTmcMap)
	}
	if _, ok := client.rpcDialPoolMap[dialPoolGroup]; !ok {
		client.rpcDialPoolMap[dialPoolGroup] = make(addrTmcMap)
	}
	m := client.rpcDialPoolMap[dialPoolGroup]
	entry, ok := m[addr]
	if !ok {
		entry = &tmcEntry{}
		m[addr] = entry
	}
	*client.dedicatedMu.Unlock()* <====< unlock here before doing a createTmc call

	// Initialize connection exactly once, without holding the mutex
	entry.once.Do(func() {
		entry.tmc, entry.err = client.createTmc(ctx, addr, opt)
	})

	if entry.err != nil {
		return nil, nil, entry.err
	}

	invalidator := func() {
		client.dedicatedMu.Lock()
		defer client.dedicatedMu.Unlock()
		if entry.tmc != nil && entry.tmc.cc != nil {
			entry.tmc.cc.Close()
		}
		delete(m, addr)
	}
	return entry.tmc.client, invalidator, nil
}
```


### Binary Version

```sh
Vitess V21
```

### Operating System and Environment details

```sh
Darwin 24.5.0
arm64
```

### Log Fragments

```sh

```
vitessio/vitess#18930.title: [Throttler] Constant throttling of vreplication flow when cluster has large number of replicas

vitessio/vitess#18937.body:
### Overview of the Issue

The TablesToCopy VStream flag will always trigger a copy even if a valid GTID is passed in.

This is a bug because it means that a VStream config has a race condition for the caller (they must pass in the config, then they must unset it before the copy completes, otherwise it will trigger another copy after that copy is complete)

The behavior should instead be to first
1. Determine if any copies should be done (with empty string gtid, or with lastPK)
2. If a copy should be performed, then use tables to copy to filter the tables



### Reproduction Steps

Create a table
Stream some changes
Set tables to copy and resume from a previous GTID
Copy performed even when valid start pos passed in

### Binary Version

```sh
vtgate version Version: 24.0.0-SNAPSHOT
```

```sh
$ uname -sr
Darwin 24.6.0
```

vitessio/vitess#18952.body:
### Overview of the Issue

`pushDerived` in `go/vt/vtgate/planbuilder/operators/route_planning.go` currently uses an explicit check for `engine.EqualUnique` opcodes, which means that other single shard routing opcodes will not be merged together.

Using `isSingleShard()` would allow more queries to be merged and pushed down to MySQL.

An example query that wouldn't be merged today would be an outer query targeting a sharded keyspace, containing a subquery that's comprised of a union of two or more queries that fetch data from an unsharded keyspace. In this case, the inner `UNION` would be mergeable, but won't be merged together today.

### Reproduction Steps


vitessio/vitess#18973.body:
## Description

The VDiff summary query can fail with "Out of sort memory, consider increasing server sort buffer size" when a VDiff involves many tables with large report JSON blobs.

The problematic query is `sqlVDiffSummary` in `go/vt/vttablet/tabletmanager/vdiff/schema.go`:

```sql
select vd.state as vdiff_state, vd.last_error as last_error, vdt.table_name as table_name,
       vd.vdiff_uuid as 'uuid', vdt.state as table_state, vdt.table_rows as table_rows,
       vd.started_at as started_at, vdt.rows_compared as rows_compared, vd.completed_at as completed_at,
       IF(vdt.mismatch = 1, 1, 0) as has_mismatch, vdt.report as report
from _vt.vdiff as vd left join _vt.vdiff_table as vdt on (vd.id = vdt.vdiff_id)
where vd.id = %a order by table_name
```

The `ORDER BY table_name` clause causes MySQL to perform a filesort that includes the potentially large JSON `report` column. When there are hundreds of tables, this can exceed the default 256KB `sort_buffer_size`.

## Root Cause

The `ORDER BY` is unnecessary because:
1. The query results are stored into Go maps keyed by table name
2. Go map iteration order is non-deterministic, so the SQL ordering provides no value
3. The text template output iterates over these maps, so it was never actually sorted consistently

## Proposed Fix

1. Remove the `ORDER BY table_name` clause from the SQL query
2. Add a `SortedTableSummaries()` method to the `Summary` struct that returns table summaries sorted alphabetically
3. Update the text templates to use this method instead of iterating over the map directly

This preserves the alphabetically sorted output in text mode while eliminating the MySQL sort buffer memory issue.
vitessio/vitess#18973.title: VDiff summary query can fail with 'Out of sort memory' error

vitessio/vitess#18984.body:
### Overview of the Issue

When initializing a VDiff, the target primary tablet will panic/crash if one of the workflow's tables has been dropped. For example:
```
I1202 21:37:50.292979       1 controller.go:117] Run finished for vdiff a35fef67-2899-4c57-951b-d87bf48100bf
panic: runtime error: index out of range [0] with length 0

goroutine 553 [running]:
vitess.io/vitess/go/vt/vttablet/tabletmanager/vdiff.(*tableDiffer).getSourcePKCols(0xc000043680)
	vitess.io/vitess/go/vt/vttablet/tabletmanager/vdiff/table_differ.go:950 +0x10d8
vitess.io/vitess/go/vt/vttablet/tabletmanager/vdiff.(*workflowDiffer).buildPlan(0xc000b3d830, {0x37eee30, 0xc000a932a8}, 0xc000dc47e0, 0xc000fe2aa0)
	vitess.io/vitess/go/vt/vttablet/tabletmanager/vdiff/workflow_differ.go:441 +0x81d
vitess.io/vitess/go/vt/vttablet/tabletmanager/vdiff.(*workflowDiffer).diff(0xc000b3d830, {0x37d8e70, 0xc00067e230})
	vitess.io/vitess/go/vt/vttablet/tabletmanager/vdiff/workflow_differ.go:323 +0x379
vitess.io/vitess/go/vt/vttablet/tabletmanager/vdiff.(*controller).start(0xc0003ef3b0, {0x37d8e70, 0xc00067e230}, {0x37eee30, 0xc0010258d8})
	vitess.io/vitess/go/vt/vttablet/tabletmanager/vdiff/controller.go:254 +0x953
vitess.io/vitess/go/vt/vttablet/tabletmanager/vdiff.(*controller).run(0xc0003ef3b0, {0x37d8e70, 0xc00067e230})
	vitess.io/vitess/go/vt/vttablet/tabletmanager/vdiff/controller.go:143 +0x52d
created by vitess.io/vitess/go/vt/vttablet/tabletmanager/vdiff.(*Engine).addController in goroutine 69
	vitess.io/vitess/go/vt/vttablet/tabletmanager/vdiff/engine.go:229 +0x1f0
```

Upon investigation, we could see that one of the tables in the workflow had been `DROP`ped in the source keyspace which then lead to this. So we need to make VDiff more robust in such cases.

h/t to @FancyFane for the investigation. ❤️ 

### Reproduction Steps

```
git checkout main && make build

cd examples/local

alias vtctldclient='command vtctldclient --server=localhost:15999'

./101_initial_cluster.sh && ./201_customer_tablets.sh && ./202_move_tables.sh

commerce_primary_uid=$(vtctldclient GetTablets --keyspace commerce --tablet-type primary --shard "0" | awk '{print $1}' | cut -d- -f2 | bc)

mysql -u root --socket "${VTDATAROOT}/vt_0000000${commerce_primary_uid}/mysql.sock" vt_commerce -e "drop table corder"

vtctldclient vdiff create --target-keyspace customer --workflow commerce2customer

mysql -e "show vitess_tablets"
```

You will see that the target/customer primary tablet is not healthy (as the `vttablet` process is gone):
```
❯ mysql -e "show vitess_tablets"
+-------+----------+-------+------------+-------------+------------------+-----------+----------------------+
| Cell  | Keyspace | Shard | TabletType | State       | Alias            | Hostname  | PrimaryTermStartTime |
+-------+----------+-------+------------+-------------+------------------+-----------+----------------------+
| zone1 | commerce | 0     | PRIMARY    | SERVING     | zone1-0000000100 | localhost | 2025-12-03T17:08:36Z |
| zone1 | commerce | 0     | REPLICA    | SERVING     | zone1-0000000101 | localhost |                      |
| zone1 | commerce | 0     | RDONLY     | SERVING     | zone1-0000000102 | localhost |                      |
| zone1 | customer | 0     | PRIMARY    | NOT_SERVING | zone1-0000000200 | localhost | 2025-12-03T17:08:51Z |
| zone1 | customer | 0     | REPLICA    | SERVING     | zone1-0000000201 | localhost |                      |
| zone1 | customer | 0     | RDONLY     | SERVING     | zone1-0000000202 | localhost |                      |
+-------+----------+-------+------------+-------------+------------------+-----------+----------------------+
```

vitessio/vitess#19008.body:
### Overview of the Issue

The vstreams (row streamers) that run on each source and target shard tablet are run asynchronously in goroutines: 
  - https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/blob/3dd1516dca6d2534cde39d77174cf9b9d091f4d6/go/vt/vttablet/tabletmanager/vdiff/table_differ.go#L162-L170
  - https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/blob/3dd1516dca6d2534cde39d77174cf9b9d091f4d6/go/vt/vttablet/tabletmanager/vdiff/table_differ.go#L303-L320
  - https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/blob/3dd1516dca6d2534cde39d77174cf9b9d091f4d6/go/vt/vttablet/tabletmanager/vdiff/table_differ.go#L322-L343

We have GTID channel which returns the GTID snapshot used for the diff. If an error occurs when generating the snapshot transaction and its associated GTID snapshot metadata, an error is returned and we handle that there.

But if an error occurs in the VStream once it's up and running, that is never passed back to the table differ (goroutine). It's only set on the shard stream struct here: https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/blob/3dd1516dca6d2534cde39d77174cf9b9d091f4d6/go/vt/vttablet/tabletmanager/vdiff/table_differ.go#L413

But we don't ever do anything with that. We just end that shard stream. Then when we are later doing the diff of the table, it's seen as though the vstream from that tablet simply produced no rows. The error is never propagated or handled. So in the end, all of the rows successfully returned from the other side are treated as extra rows.

### Reproduction Steps

```
# Setup initial cluster
cd examples/local
alias vtctldclient='command vtctldclient --server=localhost:15999'
./101_initial_cluster.sh


# Load data in the customer table
table_file="${VTDATAROOT}/vt_0000000100/data/vt_commerce/customer.ibd"
commerce_primary_uid=$(vtctldclient GetTablets --keyspace commerce --tablet-type primary --shard "0" | awk '{print $1}' | cut -d- -f2 | bc)

# Generate 2MiB of initial data
size=$((2*1024*1024))
while [[ $(stat -f "%z" "${table_file}") -lt ${size} ]]; do
    command mysql -u root --socket "${VTDATAROOT}/vt_0000000${commerce_primary_uid}/mysql.sock" vt_commerce -e "insert into customer (customer_id, email) values (${RANDOM}*${RANDOM}, '${RANDOM}_person@planetscale.com')" 2> /dev/null
done

say "Initial data load completed"

# Grow that to at least 5GiB
size=$((5*1024*1024*1024))
i=1
while [[ $(stat -f "%z" "${table_file}") -lt ${size} ]]; do
    command mysql -u root --socket "${VTDATAROOT}/vt_0000000${commerce_primary_uid}/mysql.sock" vt_commerce -e "insert into customer (email) select concat(${i}, email) from customer limit 5000000"
    let i=i+1
done

say "Full data load completed"


# Setup MoveTables workflow 
./201_customer_tablets.sh && ./202_move_tables.sh

# Wait for it to finish the copy phase...
sleep 500

# Run the VDiff
vtctldclient vdiff create --target-keyspace customer --workflow commerce2customer --auto-retry=false


# KILL the VDiff query on the source/commerce rdonly tablet
commerce_rdonly_uid=$(vtctldclient GetTablets --keyspace commerce --tablet-type rdonly | awk '{print $1}' | cut -d- -f2 | bc)

sleep 10

while true; do command mysql -u root --socket "${VTDATAROOT}/vt_0000000${commerce_rdonly_uid}/mysql.sock" vt_commerce -e 'set @id = (select id from performance_schema.processlist where info like "select /*+ MAX_EXECUTION_TIME(3600000) */ customer_id, email from customer force index %"); kill @id;'; if [[ $? -eq 0 ]]; then break; fi; sleep 0.5; done

sleep 120

vtctldclient vdiff show last --target-keyspace customer --workflow commerce2customer --verbose --format json
```

### Binary Version

```sh

```

### Operating System and Environment details

```sh
N/A
```

We observed the following failure on the source tablet, which was not detected by VDiff and led all rows read on the target to be recorded as ExtraRows:

### Overview of the Issue

When creating a MoveTables operation, it is possible for a user to pass in identical source & target keyspaces, which creates a replication looping on itself. 

### Reproduction Steps

1. Create a keyspace
2. Submit a MoveTables workflow with identical target & source keyspaces
3. Use workflow show to view the looped workflow

```sh
master
```

vitessio/vitess#19017.body:
### Feature Description

When VTTablet starts up, it creates/updates a record of itself in topo. This includes the alias, hostname, etc. 

It will be useful in later features to know _when_ that was created or updated, so that we know how old the record is, etc. This can just be `time.Now().Unix()` for the time being. 

### Use Case(s)

I have some planned features that may treat REPLCIA and RDONLY databases slightly different when they are new - for example, sending mirrored queries or adding replica-routing changes that ease a replica into service. They will use this field. 

It is also useful for debugging purposes.
vitessio/vitess#19017.title: Feature Request: Add Tablet Start Time in Topo

vitessio/vitess#19026.body:
### Overview of the Issue

`--in-order` migrations logic [says](https://vitess.io/docs/24.0/user-guides/schema-changes/ddl-strategy-flags/) in a set of migrations submitted under same migration-context, migrations must be completed in-order, and if, say, 2nd of 5 in-order migrations fails, then 3rd, 4th and 5th must subsequently abort & fail: they must not run/complete because that would make them out-of-order with said 2nd migration.

Bug: "immediate operation" migrations (`CREATE`, `DROP`) do not respect the above. Continuing our example, if 4th migrations is a `DROP TABLE`, and 2nd migration fails, then 4th migration remains in `queued` state rather than transitioning to `failed` state.

First noticed while working on https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/pull/18966

### Reproduction Steps

```sh
vtctldclient ApplySchema --sql "create table t1(id int primary key); create table t2(id int primary key); create table t3(id int primary key); create table t4(id int primary key); create table t5(id int primary key); " commerce
vtctldclient ApplySchema --ddl-strategy="vitess --in-order-completion --allow-concurrent --postpone-completion" --sql "alter table t1 force;alter table t2 force;drop table if exists t3; drop table if exists t4;" commerce
vtctldclient ApplySchema --sql "alter vitess_migration '<1st migration uuid>' cancel" commerce
```

### Binary Version

```sh
v24 and below
```

### Operating System and Environment details

```sh
all
```

### Log Fragments

```sh

```
vitessio/vitess#19026.title: Bug Report: Online DDL in-order "immediate operation" migration does not fail on failed dependency

vitessio/vitess#19043.body:
### Overview of the Issue

When VDiff detects a mismatch, it will include samples of rows that differ or only exist on one side or the other. 

It will always include the primary key columns for these sample rows so that you can identify them in the database on either side and confirm if the detected difference is valid or not. And if it is, how to address it.

BUT, if the primary key contains binary columns then the output is not usable. This is because we always convert every column to a unicode string when printing the samples. So for binary columns you will have the invalid unicode char glyph at various points which prevents you from being able to use the output to uniquely identify the row. For example:
`YS�\^C �w\^Fr���\^@\^@\^@\^@`.

### Reproduction Steps

You can see this in the code here: https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/blob/749fe5412aada58daf2af1f5fc73406ae221c75b/go/vt/vttablet/tabletmanager/vdiff/report.go#L66-L93




vitessio/vitess#19059.body:
### Overview of the Issue

The warming-reads feature introduced in https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/issues/13205 works by copying over a random percent of select queries to warm the buffer pools to improve performance of new replicas. However, SELECT...FOR UPDATE queries are not supported on read-only MySQL servers including replicas, which results in errors present in the logs

### Reproduction Steps

1. Enable warming reads on a keyspace by setting --warming-reads-percent
2. Create a new replica (or replace an existing one)
3. Run a handful of SELECT...FOR UPDATE queries on the primary
4. See the error message in the logs

### Binary Version

```sh
N/A
```

### Operating System and Environment details

```sh
MYSQL_VERSION=8.0.33
Linux 6.1.109-hs83.el9.x86_64
```

### Log Fragments

```sh
Error 1290: The MySQL server is running with the --read-only option so it cannot execute this statement
```
vitessio/vitess#19059.title: Bug Report: SELECT..FOR UPDATE Queries Break Warming Reads

vitessio/vitess#19061.body:
## Summary

@nickvanw and I would like to make it possible to restore VTTablets, and power `vtbackup`, using [MySQL `CLONE`](https://dev.mysql.com/doc/refman/8.0/en/clone-plugin-remote.html).

With `CLONE`,  it is possible to clone a remote "donor" MySQL server into a "recipient", overwriting the recipient's contents with the donor's.

## Requirements

In order for this to work:

* Both the recipient and donor MySQL must be running MySQL 8.0.17 or later.
* Both the recipient and donor MySQL must have `mysql_clone.so`plugin installed and `ACTIVE`.
* Cloning across point releases (e.g. 8.0.37 to 8.0.41) is not permitted before 8.0.37.
* The recipient must connect to the donor with `BACKUP_ADMIN` permissions, and have `CLONE_ADMIN` locally.
* The donor's `host:port` must be included in `@@global.clone_valid_donor_list`.
* Possibly a bug ([bugs#103206](https://bugs.mysql.com/bug.php?id=103206)), but a donor can only support a single `CLONE` at a time.
* Only InnoDB tables can be cloned, meaning `CLONE` usage must be restricted to all-InnoDB donors.

## API changes

Here are the API changes we have in mind to support this feature: 

* A new tablet can restore their shard's primary with `--clone-from-primary`, mutually exclusive with `--restore_from_backup`.
* A new tablet can restore from another non-primary tablet in the shard with `--clone-from-tablet=<tablet_alias>`, mutually exclusive with the above two options.
* An existing tablet can be restored with a `CloneTablet [--from-primary|--from-tablet=<tablet_alias>] <table_alias>` RPC.
* A primary cannot be the recipient in any of the above modalities.
* `vtbackup` will support `--clone-from-primary` and `--clone-from-tablet`, replacing the restore-from-backup-storage phase, and making the catch-up phase effectively instantaneous.

## Usage cases

**Repairing broken replica tablets**

We periodically experience a class of issues in which all replicas in shard hit errors such as `HA_ERR_FOUND_DUPP_KEY` and `HA_ERR_KEY_NOT_FOUND`, in which they all get stuck on the same GTID, unable to make progress. At least some of these issues are a result MySQL bugs like [bugs#105802](https://bugs.mysql.com/bug.php?id=105802).

When faced with these issues, restoring a new tablet from a pre-existing backup is not an option, as a new tablet would eventually reach the same GTID and fail to make progress. The current options we use are:

* To take a `vtctldclient Backup` of the primary, potentially taking downtime depending on the backup engine, and restore a new tablet.
* To take a backup some other way, e.g. with `mysqldump`, which is slow, generally mixed success, and not fun to do manually under the pressure of mounting replication lag.

`CLONE` is appealing here. It does not take the primary offline, and, once integrated into Vitess, will be faster than a backup/restore to/from object storage, and be hopefully much more reliable than manually running brittle `mysqldump | socat [replica]` incantations.

**Initializing new tablets**

One way to repair broken tablets in the situations described above to simply create new ones with `CLONE`, and then throw away the old ones or demote them to `SPARE` for analysis.

Outside of incident situations, it is appealing to be able to `CLONE` for the anticipated speed improvements. Restoring a backup from cloud object storage can be fairly fast when carefully optimized, but we expect `CLONE` to be significantly faster. Using `CLONE` will also largely or completely eliminate the catch-up-phase of replication which can be long depending on the time between backups and the limits of multi-threaded replication.

**Powering the restore phase of `vtbackup`**

Likewise, we expect to be able to cut down the total runtime of `vtbackup` by replacing the restore phase with `CLONE`, which will also largely or completely eliminate the catch-up-phase of replication. `vtbackup`, run in this mode, can be thought of a kind of "live primary backup".

Generally, we _like_ that a `vtbackup` cycle has the property of validating the previous restore. However, we occasionally run into issues where a large spike in customer activity inflates binary logs to such a degree that a regular `vtbackup` cannot complete in an acceptable time. In these situations, there is great appeal in being able to take a shortcut in order to satisfy a backup SLA.
vitessio/vitess#19061.title: Feature Request: MySQL CLONE for vtbackup and vttablet restore

vitessio/vitess#19080.body:
When a keyspace is deleted, `SHOW VITESS_SHARDS` fails because the keyspace name is still cached but fetching shards returns a `topo.NoNode` error.

The fix is to skip keyspaces that return `NoNode` errors instead of failing.

Related to #7955 (deleted keyspace state not reflecting in vtgate without restart).
vitessio/vitess#19080.title: SHOW VITESS_SHARDS fails when a keyspace has been deleted

vitessio/vitess#19087.body:
👋  Build problem found downstream in Homebrew (I am a maintainer):
* https://github.com/Homebrew/homebrew-core/pull/258912

Confirmed locally via simple `go build` command inside this repository:
```
$ go1.26rc1 build ./...
# vitess.io/vitess/go/hack
go/hack/ensure_swiss_map.go:39:38: invalid array length -1 (untyped int constant)
```

The Swiss-maps-ensuring Go build flag:
https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/blob/b3666b2d0e3c1809d95c906b0b15e4f7499ba951/go/hack/ensure_swiss_map.go#L1
is no longer good enough for Go 1.26, as in Go 1.26 and later Swiss maps are enabled by default and there is no more `GOEXPERIMENT=noswissmap` nor `GOEXPERIMENT=swissmap` flag either. 

See:
* https://github.com/golang/go/commit/2ae059ccaf982c3304fae0b48c1d78ad7192cbdd
* https://github.com/golang/go/issues/54766
vitessio/vitess#19087.title: Build fails with Go 1.26 rc1

vitessio/vitess#19097.body:
## Background

### Routing rules

Vitess supports routing rules for tables, allowing queries targeting one table in a keyspace to be transparently routed to another table in another (or the same) keyspace. This is used during `MoveTables` workflows for example, to shift traffic between keyspaces as tables are moved.

In order to support moving views in `MoveTables`, Vitess needs support for routing rules for views.

### Views

Views in Vitess are not explicitly defined in the VSchema. VTGates discover views dynamically through schema tracking, and are stored in the [VTGate's local VSchema](https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/blob/4a71a16deaed41c94078c50cb93549da9d9c426e/go/vt/vtgate/vindexes/vschema.go#L273). When a view changes, VTTablets notify VTGates through healthchecks, which VTGate then fetches the views' definitions with the [`GetSchema` RPC](https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/blob/4a71a16deaed41c94078c50cb93549da9d9c426e/go/vt/vttablet/queryservice/queryservice.go#L134).

At query time, when a VTGate encounters a view, the view is [replaced with its definition as a derived table](https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/blob/4a71a16deaed41c94078c50cb93549da9d9c426e/go/vt/sqlparser/normalizer.go#L630-L634). For example, for this view:

```sql
create view user_view as select id, name from user
```

A query like:

```sql
select * from user_view
```

Would be rewritten to:

```sql
select * from (select id, name from user) as user_view
```

See the [Views RFC](https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/issues/11559) for more information.

## Proposal

View routing rules can be added in the same way as table routing rules:

```json
{
  "rules": [
    {
      "from_table": "my_view",
      "to_tables": ["target_ks.my_view"]
    }
  ]
}
```

[When VTGates are building the routing rules](https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/blob/815f550356a008bfa4257a7cef957e0fc159564f/go/vt/vtgate/vindexes/vschema.go#L1012), they'll first look up the target to see if it's a view. If the view does not exist, it'll treat it as a table as normal. If the view does exist, it'll store the routing rule as a new `ViewRoutingRule`:


```go
type ViewRoutingRule struct {
	TargetKeyspace string
	TargetViewName string
}
```

For example, the rule shown above would be stored as:

```go
ViewRoutingRule{
  TargetKeyspace: "target_ks",
  TargetViewName: "customer_view"
}
```

The view routing rules would then be stored in a new map within the VTGate's [`VSchema`](https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/blob/815f550356a008bfa4257a7cef957e0fc159564f/go/vt/vtgate/vindexes/vschema.go#L64):


```go
type VSchema struct {
  // ...

	RoutingRules map[string]*RoutingRule `json:"routing_rules"`

	ViewRoutingRules map[string]*ViewRoutingRule `json:"view_routing_rules"`
    
  // ...
}
```


> [!NOTE]
> The reason we check if it's a view _before_ we check if it's a table is because tables that do not exist in the VSchema of an unsharded keyspace are assumed to be a table regardless. If we check for the reference as a table first, we would always treat it as a table even if there might exist a view with the same name.

Since views are discovered dynamically, the view might not yet exist in the target keyspace's (VTGate-local) VSchema. For example, in the case of a theoretical `MoveTables` with views:

1. `MoveTables create` initiated with view `customer_view`
2. `customer_view` is created in the target keyspace
3. Before VTGate is aware this view now exists in the target keyspace, `MoveTables` creates the routing rules
4. The VTGate receives notice of the new routing rules, but can't find a view (or table) with the same name in the target keyspace. It stores an error in its local routing rules.

    ```json
    {
      "routing_rules": {
        "customer_view": "table customer_view not found"
      }
    }
    ```

5. Soon after, the schema tracker is now aware of the new view, but the routing rules are not updated.

To work around this, whenever [new or updated views are added to the VSchema by the schema tracker](https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/blob/815f550356a008bfa4257a7cef957e0fc159564f/go/vt/vtgate/vschema_manager.go#L220), VTGate will rebuild the routing rules so that they can consider the new views.

### Query routing

At query time, views are currently [replaced with their definition as a derived table](https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/blob/4a71a16deaed41c94078c50cb93549da9d9c426e/go/vt/sqlparser/normalizer.go#L630-L634). We can update this logic to first look up the view routing rules. If a matching routing rule is found, replace the view with the _target view's_ definition instead. For example, in keyspace `source_ks` and `target_ks`, the same view is created:

```sql
create view user_view as select id, name from user
```

This routing rule:


```json
{
  "rules": [
    {
      "from_table": "user_view",
      "to_tables": ["target_ks.user_view"]
    }
  ]
}
```

Would cause this query:

```sql
select * from user_view
```

To be rewritten to:

```sql
select * from (select col1, col2 from target_ks.user_view) as user_view
```

...rather than `...from source_ks.user_view`.

## Alternatives

- Since queries containing a view are rewritten with their definition, we could choose to rely on the underlying tables' routing rules instead of creating explicit routing rules for the views themselves. However, in the case of a `MoveTables`, this would cause an ambiguous table reference when using global routing, as the view would exist in both keyspaces. Therefore, we require explicit routing rules to disambiguate.
- In VTGate, we choose to store only the target keyspace and target view name, rather than the target view's definition. The view's definition is then resolved at query time. Alternatively, we can store the target view's definition directly, which would require updating the routing rule when the view's definition changes.
- We can create an entirely new field in VSchema for view routing rules. This means also creating the equivalent `ApplyRoutingRules`, etc. commands. This approach would allow us to be more explicit instead of trying to treat views and tables similarly, but requires more effort up front.

## Implications

- The underlying tables that views reference likely have their own routing rules as well. When views are replaced with the target view's definition, the tables will still have their own routing applied as well. In the case of `MoveTables`, the routing rules will be configured in the same direction, but in the case of manual routing rules, there might be some conflicting routing if users are not careful.
vitessio/vitess#19097.title: RFC: View routing rules

vitessio/vitess#19101.body:
### Motivation: Hot Reloading

When updating Vitess services, it is necessary to shift traffic from the old process to the new process. In some cases, it may be convenient and advantageous to do so without requiring a traffic shift to a different host:port for the new version, but instead to migrate the version in place on the same host or container.

### Proposal

Add an option for ports to be bound with `SO_REUSEPORT` on unix builds of Vitess. `SO_REUSEPORT` [is a unix socket option](https://lwn.net/Articles/542629/) which allows multiple processes running under the same UID to bind the same port at the same time. The kernel then load balances traffic between all processes listening on the port. This option can be utilized by users who want to update Vitess binaries in place without service interruption, or to hot-reload configuration.

[WIP implementation](https://github.com/slackhq/vitess/compare/main...slackhq:vitess:rlaine-reuseport?expand=1)

### Alternative

Alternatively, create a new top level attribute, `servenv.Listen` with the type `func Listen(network, address string) (net.Listener, error)`, and then replace calls to `net.Listen` with `servenv.Listen`. Users could override this attribute with a custom implementation. This would allow users to inject their own OS/config-specific socket binding functions, while keeping the Vitess repo minimal.

[Alternative implementation](https://github.com/slackhq/vitess/compare/main...slackhq:vitess:rlaine-override-listen?expand=1)

### Use Case(s)

At Slack, we run [a VTGate-like sidecar daemon](https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/issues/16045) along side a web application via systemd. We would like to be able to configure our web applications with a static host/port (eg. `localhost:3306`) and then perform sidecar updates in-place without service disruption.
vitessio/vitess#19101.title: RFC: SO_REUSEPORT

vitessio/vitess#19103.body:
### Overview of the Issue

The `binparserOpaque` function in `go/mysql/binlog/binlog_json.go` incorrectly parses opaque JSON values (DATE, TIME, DATETIME, DECIMAL, BIT, BLOB, STRING, etc.) using hardcoded offsets:

```go
start := 3       // assumed fixed 2-byte length prefix
end := start + 8 // assumed all opaque values are 8 bytes
```

In MySQL binary JSON opaque values use variable-length encoding.

This causes:
1. **Panics** when opaque values are smaller than 8 bytes (e.g., small decimals)
2. **Incorrect data** for BIT/BLOB/STRING opaque types — the length byte was incorrectly included as part of the value

Affected operations include VStream/CDC and VReplication (MoveTables, Reshard, Online DDL) on tables with JSON columns containing opaque types.

### Reproduction Steps

1. Create a table with a JSON column containing small opaque values:
   ```sql
   CREATE TABLE t (id INT PRIMARY KEY, j JSON);
   INSERT INTO t VALUES (1, CAST(1.99 AS DECIMAL(3,2)));
   ```

2. Set up VStream or VReplication on this table

3. Observe panic in vttablet:
   ```
   panic: runtime error: slice bounds out of range [:11] with capacity 7
   ```

### Binary Version

```sh
Vitess 22
```

### Operating System and Environment details

```sh
Ubuntu Linux
```

### Log Fragments

```sh
panic: runtime error: slice bounds out of range [:11] with capacity 7
vitess.io/vitess/go/vt/vttablet/tabletserver/vstreamer.(*vstreamer).processRowEvent(0xc00b0ca360, {0x0, 0x0, 0x0}, 0xc00eb043a0, {0x1, {{0x0, 0x0, 0x0}, 0x0}, ...})
vitess.io/vitess/go/vt/vttablet/tabletserver/vstreamer/vstreamer.go:1089 +0x2d7
vitess.io/vitess/go/vt/vttablet/tabletserver/vstreamer.(*vstreamer).parseEvent(0xc00b0ca360, {0x3efeef0, 0xc031f15c60}, 0x0)
vitess.io/vitess/go/vt/vttablet/tabletserver/vstreamer/vstreamer.go:702 +0x2173
vitess.io/vitess/go/vt/vttablet/tabletserver/vstreamer.(*vstreamer).parseEvent(0xc00b0ca360, {0x3efeef0, 0xc025b2be20}, 0xc009087d68)
vitess.io/vitess/go/vt/vttablet/tabletserver/vstreamer/vstreamer.go:729 +0x2df0
vitess.io/vitess/go/vt/vttablet/tabletserver/vstreamer.(*vstreamer).parseEvents(0xc00b0ca360, {0x3ec1ab0, 0xc009ca3e50}, 0xc0012f4af0, 0xc0012f4b60)
vitess.io/vitess/go/vt/vttablet/tabletserver/vstreamer/vstreamer.go:408 +0x8ac
vitess.io/vitess/go/vt/vttablet/tabletserver/vstreamer.(*vstreamer).replicate(0xc00b0ca360, {0x305?, 0xc003ff6c00?})
vitess.io/vitess/go/vt/vttablet/tabletserver/vstreamer/vstreamer.go:222 +0x10c
vitess.io/vitess/go/vt/vttablet/tabletserver/vstreamer.(*vstreamer).Stream(0xc00b0ca360)
vitess.io/vitess/go/vt/vttablet/tabletserver/vstreamer/vstreamer.go:201 +0x185
vitess.io/vitess/go/vt/vttablet/tabletserver/vstreamer.(*uvstreamer).catchup.func2()
vitess.io/vitess/go/vt/vttablet/tabletserver/vstreamer/copy.go:80 +0x19a
created by vitess.io/vitess/go/vt/vttablet/tabletserver/vstreamer.(*uvstreamer).catchup in goroutine 2412582097
vitess.io/vitess/go/vt/vttablet/tabletserver/vstreamer/copy.go:75 +0x178
```
vitessio/vitess#19103.title: Bug Report: binlog_json opaque value parsing uses hardcoded sizes instead of variable-length encoding

vitessio/vitess#19113.body:
### Overview of the Issue

When executing a query that joins a table with a derived table containing a UNION that spans multiple keyspaces (or shards), the query planner fails with the error:


```
VT13001: [BUG] could not find the column '...' on the UNION
```

### Reproduction Steps

This can be reproduced with the following query running against the planner test vschema.

```sql
-- Where `user` is in a sharded keyspace and `unsharded` is in an unsharded keyspace.
SELECT u.id
FROM user AS u
JOIN (
    SELECT id AS pid FROM user WHERE id = 1
    UNION
    SELECT id AS pid FROM unsharded WHERE id = 1
) AS i ON u.id = i.pid
```

### Binary Version

This affects v22, v23 and `main`.

### Operating System and Environment details

```sh
N/A
```

### Log Fragments

```sh
N/A
```
vitessio/vitess#19113.title: Bug Report: Query planning fails for `JOIN`s with derived tables containing `UNION` across keyspaces/shards

vitessio/vitess#19115.body:
### Overview of the Issue

MoveTablesCreate can incorrectly revert a sharded target keyspace VSchema to `nil` on failures. 

This regressed after PR #17401: origVSchema was initialized as non-nil with only a Name, so the deferred rollback always attempted to SaveVSchema even for sharded targets where no VSchema mutation should occur. When that rollback happens, Keyspace is nil and the VSchema gets cleared.

Impact: Any error after vreplication streams are created (for example, during routing rules setup, denied tables setup, or later validation) can wipe the target VSchema on sharded keyspaces, leading to incorrect routing and missing vindex/table definitions.

### Reproduction Steps

Start a MoveTables operation in Vitess 22+ that fails with a sharded keyspace. 

The regression tests to be introduced will also demonstrate how to trigger this.

### Binary Version

```sh
Vitess 22+
```

### Operating System and Environment details

```sh
N/A
```

### Log Fragments

```sh
2026-01-09 19:30:51.154infoI0109 19:30:51.153964       1 vschema.go:72] successfully updated vschema for keyspace blah: <nil>
```
vitessio/vitess#19115.title: Bug Report: Sharded target VSchema cleared by MoveTablesCreate error path

vitessio/vitess#19122.body:
### Overview of the Issue

VReplication table vreplication use `varbinary(10000)` for GTID position columns (`pos` and `stop_pos`). This size limitation causes "Data too long for column" errors when GTID position data exceeds 10,000 bytes.
I propose to change the column type from `varbinary(10000)` to `longblob` to accommodate GTID positions of any practical size (up to 4GB).

### Reproduction Steps

1. run schema-change by vitess onlineddl with `vitess --declarative` strategy
2. failed Due to ` _vt.vreplication.pos Column Size Limiterror message: Data too long for column 'pos' at row 1 (errno 1406) during query: update _vt.vreplication set pos...`

### Binary Version

```sh
19.0.7
```

### Operating System and Environment details

```sh
Ubuntu
```

### Log Fragments

```sh

```
vitessio/vitess#19122.title: Bug Report: VReplication table GTID position columns size limit is insufficient

vitessio/vitess#19133.body:
### Overview of the Issue

When using `WorkflowMirrorTraffic` with `percent=0` to remove mirror traffic for a specific workflow, the code incorrectly deletes all mirror rules for a table, including unrelated rules pointing to other keyspaces.

The bug is in `go/vt/vtctl/workflow/traffic_switcher.go` in the `mirrorTableTraffic` function. When removing a mirror rule (percent=0), the code does:
```go
delete(mrs, fromTable)
```

This deletes the entire map entry for `fromTable`, wiping out **all** mirror targets for that table. The correct behavior should only delete the specific `toTable` entry:
```go
delete(mrs[fromTable], toTable)
if len(mrs[fromTable]) == 0 {
    delete(mrs, fromTable)
}
```

For example, if table `source.t1` has mirror rules to both `target.t1` (50%) and `other.t1` (25%), and you run `WorkflowMirrorTraffic` with `percent=0` for the `target` workflow, it should only remove the `target.t1` rule while preserving the `other.t1` rule. Instead, it deletes both.

### Reproduction Steps

1. Set up mirror rules for a table pointing to multiple target keyspaces:
   ```json
   {
     "source.table1": {
       "target.table1": 50.0,
       "other.table1": 25.0
     }
   }
   ```

2. Run `WorkflowMirrorTraffic` with `percent=0` to remove mirroring for the `target` workflow:
   ```
   vtctldclient WorkflowMirrorTraffic --keyspace target --workflow src2target --percent 0
   ```

3. Expected result: Mirror rules should be:
   ```json
   {
     "source.table1": {
       "other.table1": 25.0
     }
   }
   ```

4. Actual result: All mirror rules for `source.table1` are deleted, including the unrelated `other.table1` rule.

### Binary Version

```
All versions containing the mirror traffic feature (introduced in v21+)
```

### Operating System and Environment details

```
N/A - this is a code logic bug, not environment-specific
```

### Log Fragments

```
No specific logs - the bug is silent data loss in the mirror rules configuration
```
vitessio/vitess#19133.title: Bug Report: WorkflowMirrorTraffic with percent=0 incorrectly deletes unrelated mirror rules

vitessio/vitess#19134.body:
## Overview of the Issue

When creating a SNAPSHOT keyspace using `vtctl CreateKeyspace` with `-keyspace_type=snapshot`, if the base keyspace has no VSchema (returns a `NoNode` error from topology), the code panics with a nil pointer dereference.

The bug is in `go/vt/vtctl/vtctl.go` around line 1907. When `GetVSchema` fails with `NoNode`, the code correctly creates an empty vschema in the error handling block, but then unconditionally attempts to clone from `bksvs.Keyspace` outside the if/else block. Since `bksvs` is nil when `GetVSchema` returns an error, this causes a panic.

The equivalent code in `grpcvtctldserver/server.go` handles this correctly by reassigning `bksvs` in the error case before cloning.

## Reproduction Steps

1. Create a base keyspace without a VSchema:
   ```sh
   vtctl CreateKeyspace base_ks
   ```

2. Attempt to create a SNAPSHOT keyspace based on it:
   ```sh
   vtctl CreateKeyspace -keyspace_type=snapshot -base_keyspace=base_ks -snapshot_time="2024-01-01T00:00:00Z" snapshot_ks
   ```

3. Observe nil pointer panic when the code attempts to call `bksvs.Keyspace.CloneVT()` with a nil `bksvs`.

## Binary Version

All versions on main branch (code inspection - the bug exists in the current codebase at `go/vt/vtctl/vtctl.go:1907`)

## Operating System and Environment details

N/A - this is a code logic bug, not environment-specific.

## Log Fragments

```
panic: runtime error: invalid memory address or nil pointer dereference
[signal SIGSEGV: segmentation violation]

goroutine X [running]:
vitess.io/vitess/go/vt/vtctl.commandCreateKeyspace(...)
    go/vt/vtctl/vtctl.go:1907
```
vitessio/vitess#19134.title: Bug Report: Nil pointer panic in vtctl CreateKeyspace for SNAPSHOT keyspace when base keyspace has no VSchema

vitessio/vitess#19140.body:
### Overview of the Issue

Running a Vitess v22 workflow (reference tables) with Percona MySQL 8.0.43-34 and semi-sync enabled resulted in a cluster-wide failure after triggering VDiff.

Some tables were not copied during workflow initialization (source had rows, target had zero rows). When VDiff was started, primary tablets entered a crashloop due to a `panic: runtime error: integer divide by zero`. After that, workflows became inaccessible via both vtadmin and vtctldclient across the entire cluster.

The cluster could only be recovered by manually deleting all VDiff-related records from the `_vt` database.


### Reproduction Steps

1. Create a Vitess v22 workflow involving reference tables.
2. Ensure some source tables contain data that was inserted **before** the available binlog retention window.
3. Delete the existing workflow.
4. Truncate or clear target tables.
5. Create a new workflow.
6. Observe that some tables are not copied:
   - Source table has rows
   - Target table has zero rows
7. Run VDiff on the workflow using vtadmin.
8. Observe primary tablets starting to crashloop.
9. Attempt to access workflows:
   - vtadmin UI does not load workflows
   - `vtctldclient` cannot list, stop, or delete workflows or VDiff
10. Check vttablet logs and observe a panic similar to:
    ```
    panic: runtime error: integer divide by zero
    ```

### Binary Version

```sh
Version: 22.0.2 (Git revision ef39c169b74f88563f0c2d9b6504807e54b8d52c branch 'HEAD') built on Wed Nov  5 17:16:04 UTC 2025 by vitess@buildkitsandbox using go1.24.9 linux/amd64
```

### Operating System and Environment details

```sh
$ cat /etc/os-release 
PRETTY_NAME="Debian GNU/Linux 12 (bookworm)"
NAME="Debian GNU/Linux"
VERSION_ID="12"
VERSION="12 (bookworm)"
VERSION_CODENAME=bookworm
ID=debian
HOME_URL="https://www.debian.org/"
```

vitessio/vitess#19147.body:
### Problem

The GetSnakeName function in go/stats/snake_case_converter.go is used to convert CamelCase metric names to snake_case (e.g., for Prometheus). It uses a memoization cache to avoid recomputing the same conversions.

**The current implementation uses a regular sync.Mutex**, which serializes all access to the cache—even read-only lookups. Since metric names are typically registered once and then read repeatedly during the lifetime of a process, this creates unnecessary contention:

https://github.com/vitessio/vitess/blob/ef25e5b450344b3ca0a187f335711cf773d916f8/go/stats/snake_case_converter.go#L36-L41 

In high-throughput scenarios with many goroutines accessing stats, this becomes a bottleneck—multiple goroutines block each other just to look up already-cached values.

### Solution
Replace sync.Mutex with sync.RWMutex and implement a double-checked locking pattern:
  1. Fast path: Acquire read lock (RLock) and check cache—multiple goroutines can do this concurrently
  2. Slow path: If not cached, acquire write lock (Lock) to compute and store
  3. Double-check: After acquiring write lock, re-check cache (another goroutine may have computed it while we waited)

**This is a classic read-heavy cache optimization pattern.**

### Expected Impact 
- Read-heavy workloads (the common case): Multiple goroutines can read cached values concurrently without blocking each other
- Write path: Unchanged—still serialized for safety
- Memory: No change
- Correctness: Concurrent test added with 100 goroutines × 1000 iterations
vitessio/vitess#19147.title: Optimize GetSnakeName with RWMutex for concurrent reads

Inserting JSON that has worked for almost the full history of Vitess now throws an error. I tried rolling back vtgate first, and that had no effect. Rolling back vttablet resolved the issue.

`
target: workspaces.0.primary: vttablet: rpc error: code = Unknown desc = Cannot create a JSON value from a string with CHARACTER SET 'binary'. (errno 3144) (sqlstate 22032) (CallerID: unsecure_grpc_client): Sql: "insert into workspaces_rankings__pulls_by_team(workspace_id, team_id, pull_id, ranking_id, requested, keyword_id, keyword_sources) values (:v1, :v2, :v3, :v4, :v5, :v6, :v7)", BindVars: {v1: "type:INT64 value:\"15644401301101\""v2: "type:INT64 value:\"910629795084904\""v3: "type:INT64 value:\"2778530002\""v4: "type:INT64 value:\"2783412902\""v5: "type:INT64 value:\"1768867200\""v6: "type:INT64 value:\"101323530\""v7: "type:VARBINARY value:\"[]\""}

```sql
) ENGINE=InnoDB AUTO_INCREMENT=3 DEFAULT CHARSET=utf8mb4 COLLATE=utf8mb4_unicode_ci
```
